title: Amazon incident
description: Elastic Load Balancer ran into problems when "a maintenance process that was inadvertently run against the production ELB state data".
symptoms: Engineers observed high latency and error rates for API calls, performance issues with running load balancers, and degraded performance and errors for customer applications using modified load balancers.
root_cause: A maintenance process was inadvertently run against the production ELB state data, resulting in the logical deletion of a portion of this data.
resolution: The ELB technical team disabled several of the ELB control plane workflows to prevent additional service impact and focused on recovering the missing ELB state data.
summary: The Amazon ELB service experienced high latency and errors due to the inadvertent deletion of critical state data during a maintenance process, leading to performance issues for customer applications. The technical team disabled workflows to mitigate further impact and worked on recovering the missing data.

title: Square incident
description: A cascading error from an adjacent service lead to merchant authentication service being overloaded. This impacted merchants for ~2 hours.
symptoms: Engineers observed timeouts from the customer authentication service and reported failures in services depending on merchant identity.
root_cause: Merchant authentication service unrecoverably overloaded due to bug in adjacent service
resolution: Rolled back all software changes leading up to the incident and activated a cross-functional crisis team to follow the disaster response plan.
duration: Service outage for all merchants between 10:02AM-11:55AM; 2FA outage until 1:12PM
summary: A cascading error from an adjacent service caused the merchant authentication service to become overloaded, leading to widespread service outages. The issue was resolved by rolling back recent changes and activating a crisis team.

title: Instapaper incident
description: Also [this](https://web.archive.org/web/20240911020547/https://blog.instapaper.com/post/157027537441). Limits were hit for a hosted database. It took many hours to migrate over to a new database.
symptoms: The bookmarks table began to throw errors when attempting to insert new entries after exceeding the 2TB file size limit.
root_cause: The data failure was caused by a 2TB file size limit for RDS instances created before April 2014, specifically affecting the bookmarks table.
resolution: Migrated to a new database to resolve the file size limitation issue.
duration: From February 9 at 12:30PM PT to February 10 at 7:30PM PT.
summary: Instapaper experienced a critical outage when the bookmarks table exceeded the 2TB file size limit, causing errors on new entries. The issue was resolved by migrating to a new database, restoring full service after several hours.

title: Stackdriver incident
description: In October 2013, [Stackdriver](https://www.stackdriver.com/), experienced an outage, when its Cassandra cluster crashed. Data published by various services into a message bus was being injested into the Cassandra cluster. When the cluster failed, the failure percolated to various producers, that ended up blocking on queue insert operations, eventually leading to the failure of the entire application.
symptoms: The application failed as various producers were blocking on queue insert operations.
root_cause: The Cassandra cluster crashed, leading to a failure in data ingestion.
resolution: Not specified
summary: In October 2013, Stackdriver experienced a critical outage when its Cassandra cluster crashed, causing producers to block on queue insert operations and ultimately leading to the failure of the entire application.

title: Amazon incident
description: Human error during a routine networking upgrade led to a resource crunch, exacerbated by software bugs, that ultimately resulted in an outage across all US East Availability Zones as well as a loss of 0.07% of volumes.
symptoms: EBS volumes became unable to service read and write operations, causing instances to get 'stuck' and resulting in high error rates and latencies for EBS API calls across the US East Region.
root_cause: Human error during a routine networking upgrade led to a resource crunch, exacerbated by software bugs.
resolution: Disabled all control APIs for EBS in the affected Availability Zone to restore volumes and stabilize the EBS cluster.
summary: The incident involved EBS volumes becoming unresponsive due to human error during a networking upgrade, leading to widespread service disruption. The resolution involved disabling control APIs to restore functionality.

title: ARPANET incident
description: A malfunctioning IMP ([Interface Message Processor](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Interface_Message_Processor)) corrupted routing data, software recomputed checksums propagating bad data with good checksums, incorrect sequence numbers caused buffers to fill, full buffers caused loss of keepalive packets and nodes took themselves off the network. From 1980.
symptoms: Network appeared to be unusable, with no IMP able to communicate reliably, resulting in 'net trouble' error messages and broken connections.
root_cause: A malfunctioning IMP corrupted routing data, leading to faulty network control packets and excessive resource usage by one IMP process.
resolution: Restoring the network to operational condition was a straightforward task with no residual problems once the network was restored.
summary: The ARPANET experienced a high-severity incident where no IMP could communicate reliably due to a malfunctioning IMP that corrupted routing data. The issue was resolved by restoring the network to operational condition without any residual problems.

title: Amazon incident
description: Scaling the front-end cache fleet for Kinesis caused all of the servers in the fleet to exceed the maximum number of threads allowed by an operating system configuration. Multiple critical downstream services affected, from Cognito to Lambda to CloudWatch.
symptoms: Errors on putting and getting Kinesis records, alarms firing, and a variety of errors observed in calls made by front-end fleet members.
root_cause: Scaling the front-end cache fleet for Kinesis caused all servers in the fleet to exceed the maximum number of threads allowed by an operating system configuration.
resolution: The new capacity was removed while researching other errors, and a full restart of the front-end fleet was determined to be necessary.
summary: The incident involved errors in Kinesis record operations due to exceeding thread limits in the front-end cache fleet, leading to a critical service disruption. The resolution involved removing the new capacity and planning a full fleet restart.

title: Google incident
description: Filestore enforces a global limit on API requests to limit impact in overload scenarios. The outage was triggered when an internal Google service managing a large number of GCP projects malfunctioned and overloaded the Filestore API with requests, causing global throttling of the Filestore API. This continued until the internal service was manually paused. As a result of this throttling, read-only API access was unavailable for all customers. This affected customers in all locations, due to a global quota that applies to Filestore. Console, gcloud and API access (List, GetOperation, etc.) calls all failed for a duration of 3 hours, 12 minutes. Mutate operations (CreateInstance, UpdateInstance, CreateBackup, etc.) still succeeded, but customers were unable to check on operation progress.
symptoms: All read-only API requests failed with error code 429 globally, affecting customers in all locations. Console, gcloud, and API access calls failed for a duration of 3 hours, 12 minutes.
root_cause: An internal Google service managing a large number of GCP projects malfunctioned and overloaded the Filestore API with requests, causing global throttling.
resolution: The internal service was manually paused to stop the overload on the Filestore API.
summary: The incident involved a global failure of read-only API requests for Google Cloud Filestore due to an overload caused by an internal service malfunction. The issue was resolved by manually pausing the internal service, restoring API access.

title: TUI incident
description: Prior to the incident flight the reservation system from which the load sheet was produced had been upgraded. A fault in the system caused female passengers checked in with title ‘Miss’ to be counted as children. The system allocated them a child’s standard weight of 35 kg as opposed to the correct female standard weight of 69 kg. Consequently, with 38 females checked in incorrectly and misidentified as children, the G-TAWG takeoff mass from the load sheet was 1,244 kg below the actual mass of the aircraft.
symptoms: The reservation system incorrectly counted female passengers with the title 'Miss' as children, leading to a significant discrepancy in the aircraft's takeoff mass.
root_cause: A fault in the upgraded reservation system caused misidentification of female passengers as children.
resolution: Not specified
summary: The incident involved a fault in the reservation system that misidentified female passengers as children, resulting in a 1,244 kg discrepancy in the aircraft's takeoff mass. The issue was due to a system upgrade that incorrectly allocated child weight to these passengers.

title: Discord incident
description: "At approximately 14:01, a Redis instance acting as the primary for a highly-available cluster used by Discord's API services was migrated automatically by Google’s Cloud Platform. This migration caused the node to incorrectly drop offline, forcing the cluster to rebalance and trigger known issues with the way Discord API instances handle Redis failover. After resolving this partial outage, unnoticed issues on other services caused a cascading failure through Discord’s real time system. These issues caused enough critical impact that Discord’s engineering team was forced to fully restart the service, reconnecting millions of clients over a period of 20 minutes."
symptoms: Engineers were alerted to anomalies via real time metrics and monitoring, observed abnormal latency on API endpoints, and received end user reports of servers appearing offline.
root_cause: A Redis instance was automatically migrated by Google’s Cloud Platform, causing it to drop offline and trigger known issues with Redis failover handling in API instances.
resolution: Engineers executed a rolling restart of all API instances, corrected a misconfigured edge caching rule, disabled the problematic API endpoint, and recovered a non-critical Cassandra cluster.
summary: The incident was triggered by an automatic migration of a Redis instance, leading to failover issues and cascading failures in Discord's real time system. Engineers resolved the situation by restarting API instances and correcting configuration errors, ultimately restoring service.

title: NASA incident
description: NASA's Mars Pathfinder spacecraft experienced system resets a few days after landing on Mars (1997).  Debugging features were remotely enabled until the cause was found: a [priority inversion](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Priority_inversion) problem in the VxWorks operating system.  The OS software was remotely patched (all the way to Mars) to fix the problem by adding priority inheritance to the task scheduler.
symptoms: System resets were observed a few days after landing on Mars.
root_cause: A priority inversion problem in the VxWorks operating system.
resolution: The OS software was remotely patched by adding priority inheritance to the task scheduler.
summary: NASA's Mars Pathfinder spacecraft experienced system resets due to a priority inversion issue in the VxWorks OS. The problem was resolved by remotely patching the software to implement priority inheritance in the task scheduler.

title: Pagerduty incident
description: In April 2013, [Pagerduty](https://web.archive.org/web/20220906003007/https://www.pagerduty.com/), a cloud service proving application uptime monitoring and real-time notifications, suffered an outage when two of its three independent cloud deployments in different data centers began experiencing connectivity issues and high network latency. It was found later that the two independent deployments shared a common peering point which was experiencing network instability.  While the third deployment was still operational, Pagerduty's applications failed to establish quorum due to to high network latency and hence failed in their ability to send notifications.
symptoms: Delays in notifications and 500s on API endpoints; connectivity issues and high network latency observed.
root_cause: Two independent cloud deployments shared a common peering point which experienced network instability.
resolution: PagerDuty's notification dispatch system was able to reach quorum and started processing queued notifications after the connectivity issue ended.
summary: PagerDuty experienced critical delays in notifications due to high network latency caused by a shared peering point between two cloud deployments. The issue was resolved when the notification dispatch system regained quorum after the connectivity issue was resolved.

title: WebKit code repository incident
description: The WebKit repository, a Subversion repository configured to use deduplication, became unavailable after two files with the same SHA-1 hash were checked in as test data, with the intention of implementing a safety check for collisions. The two files had different md5 sums and so a checkout would fail a consistency check. For context, the first public SHA-1 hash collision had very recently been announced, with an example of two colliding files.
symptoms: The WebKit repository became unavailable, and checkouts failed a consistency check due to two files with the same SHA-1 hash being committed.
root_cause: Two files with differing content but the same SHA-1 hash were committed, causing a failure in the consistency check due to representation sharing in Subversion.
resolution: Not specified
summary: The WebKit repository experienced unavailability and checkout failures after two files with the same SHA-1 hash were committed. The root cause was a collision in the SHA-1 hash due to representation sharing in Subversion.

title: European Space Agency incident
description: An overflow occurred when converting a 16-bit number to a 64-bit numer in the Ariane 5 intertial guidance system, causing the rocket to crash. The actual overflow occurred in code that wasn't necessary for operation but was running anyway. According to [one account](https://web.archive.org/web/20120829114850/https://www.around.com/ariane.html), this caused a diagnostic error message to get printed out, and the diagnostic error message was somehow interpreted as actual valid data. According to [another account](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Cluster_%28spacecraft%29?oldid=217305667), no trap handler was installed for the overflow.
symptoms: The rocket veered off its flight path and was destroyed by its automated destruct system, resulting in the loss of the payload and significant financial loss.
root_cause: An arithmetic overflow occurred when converting a 64-bit floating point number to a 16-bit signed integer, compounded by the disabling of the software handler for this error trap.
resolution: Not specified
summary: The Ariane 5 rocket experienced a critical failure due to an arithmetic overflow in the control software, leading to its destruction shortly after launch. The incident highlighted significant design and management issues, as pre-flight tests did not account for the new flight conditions.

title: Amazon incident
description: At 7:30 AM PST, an automated activity to scale capacity of one of the AWS services hosted in the main AWS network triggered an unexpected behavior from a large number of clients inside the internal network. This resulted in a large surge of connection activity that overwhelmed the networking devices between the internal network and the main AWS network, resulting in delays for communication between these networks. These delays increased latency and errors for services communicating between these networks, resulting in even more connection attempts and retries. This led to persistent congestion and performance issues on the devices connecting the two networks.
symptoms: Increased latency and errors for services communicating between the internal network and the main AWS network, elevated internal DNS errors, and persistent congestion impacting real-time monitoring data.
root_cause: An automated activity to scale capacity of one of the AWS services triggered unexpected behavior from a large number of clients, overwhelming the networking devices between the internal network and the main AWS network.
resolution: The team moved the internal DNS traffic away from the congested network paths, which improved the availability of several impacted services.
summary: The incident involved increased latency and errors due to an automated scaling activity that overwhelmed networking devices, leading to congestion. The resolution involved rerouting internal DNS traffic, which improved service availability but did not fully resolve the congestion.

title: Google incident
description: A policy change containing blank fields triggered a null pointer exception in Service Control, Google's API management and control plane system. The code path that failed was not feature flag protected and lacked proper error handling. When the policy data replicated globally, it caused Service Control binaries to crash loop across all regions. While a red-button fix was deployed within 40 minutes, larger regions like us-central-1 experienced extended recovery times (up to 2h 40m) due to a thundering herd problem when Service Control tasks restarted, overloading the underlying Spanner infrastructure. The incident affected Google and Google Cloud APIs globally, with recovery times varying by product architecture.
symptoms: Service Control binaries crashed and looped across all regions, affecting multiple Google and Google Cloud APIs globally.
root_cause: A policy change containing blank fields triggered a null pointer exception in Service Control, which was not feature flag protected and lacked proper error handling.
resolution: A red-button fix was deployed within 40 minutes.
duration: Incident began at 2025-06-12 10:51 and ended at 2025-06-12 18:18, lasting 7 hours and 27 minutes.
summary: A null pointer exception caused by a policy change with blank fields led to Service Control crashes, affecting multiple APIs. A red-button fix was deployed within 40 minutes, but recovery times varied significantly due to a thundering herd problem.

title: CircleCI incident
description: A GitHub outage and recovery caused an unexpectedly large incoming load. For reasons that aren't specified, a large load causes CircleCI's queue system to slow down, in this case to handling one transaction per minute.
symptoms: Queue length was rising, but build capacity was not fully utilized; dequeuing on the order of one build per minute; long list of queued builds; queries taking a massive amount of time to run; many queries timing out.
root_cause: A severe degradation in database performance caused by a sustained surge in build requests from GitHub, leading to significant resource contention across everything accessing the same database.
resolution: Initially attempted to salvage the queue, but ultimately decided to delete the queue as the value of processing the builds dropped significantly.
summary: CircleCI experienced a critical incident where a surge in build requests from GitHub led to severe database performance degradation, causing the build queue to lock up. The resolution involved deleting the queue after attempts to salvage it were deemed ineffective.

title: Amazon incident
description: Bad weather caused power failures throughout AWS US East. A single backup generator failed to deliver stable power when power switched over to backup and the generator was loaded. This is despite having passed a load tests two months earlier, and passing weekly power-on tests.
symptoms: Power failures and instability in generator power leading to servers losing power.
root_cause: A single backup generator failed to deliver stable power when switched over, despite passing load tests.
resolution: The backup generator power was stabilized, UPS systems were restarted, and power was restored to the facility.
duration: Approximately 30 minutes from 8:04pm PDT to 8:34pm PDT.
summary: During a severe storm, a backup generator failed to provide stable power, causing servers to lose power. The issue was resolved by stabilizing the generator and restarting the UPS systems.

title: Google incident
description: A bug in configuration roll-out to a load balancer lead to increased error rates for 22 minutes.
symptoms: Requests to the Google Cloud HTTP(S) Load Balancer experienced a 25% error rate, with some Load Balancers showing 100% error rates and clients receiving 502 errors.
root_cause: A bug in the HTTP(S) Load Balancer configuration update process caused it to revert to a configuration that was substantially out of date due to a mastership change and loss of access to recent configuration files.
resolution: Google paused all configuration changes to the HTTP(S) Load Balancer for three hours and 41 minutes until the root cause was understood.
summary: The incident involved a 25% error rate for requests to the Google Cloud HTTP(S) Load Balancer due to a bug in the configuration update process, which caused a reversion to an outdated configuration. Google paused configuration changes to address the issue.

title: NPM incident
description: Fastly configuration change caused backend routing issue. To be exact, the issue is that we were setting the req.backend in a vcl_fetch function, and then calling restart to re-parse the rules. However, calling restart will reset the req.backend to the first backed in the list, which in this case happened to be Manta, rather than the load balanced CouchDB servers.
symptoms: All requests were directed to Manta, resulting in 403 Forbidden responses due to Manta's inability to handle certain requests. This led to a thundering herd effect as Fastly did not cache error codes.
root_cause: A bug in the Fastly VCL configuration change that reset req.backend to Manta instead of the intended CouchDB servers.
resolution: Identified the root cause with Fastly support, ensured req.backend is explicitly set in the vcl_recv function, and set up a separate Varnish instance for staging changes.
summary: The incident involved all requests being routed to Manta, causing 403 errors due to a configuration bug in Fastly. The root cause was identified, and steps were taken to ensure proper backend routing in future configurations.

title: Basecamp incident
description: , [see also](https://web.archive.org/web/20220530044506/https://m.signalvnoise.com/update-on-basecamp-3-being-stuck-in-read-only-as-of-nov-8-922am-cst/). In November 2018 a database hit the integer limit, leaving the service in read-only mode.
symptoms: Basecamp 3 was in read-only mode, preventing users from entering new information or altering existing information.
root_cause: The database hit the integer limit of 2,147,483,647 on the events table, which tracks almost every activity in Basecamp.
resolution: The capacity of the events table was expanded by updating the database schema to accommodate a higher limit.
summary: Basecamp experienced a critical outage where users were unable to modify or add information due to the database hitting its integer limit. The issue was resolved by expanding the events table's capacity, which had been a known problem that was not addressed in time.

duration: Approximately 25 minutes for most users, with some experiencing issues for up to 30 minutes longer.

title: Stack Exchange incident
description: The primary SQL-Server triggered a bugcheck on the SQL Server process, causing the Stack Exchange sites to go into read only mode, and eventually a complete outage.
symptoms: System degradation (read only) followed by a site outage, with the Stack Exchange network going into read-only mode for approximately 5 minutes before being offline for 12 minutes.
root_cause: The primary SQL Server (NY-SQL02) triggered a bugcheck on the SQL Server process, which initially put the SQL server into a read-only state.
resolution: A restart of the SQL service on NY-SQL02 brought the network back online in read-only mode, followed by a sanity check on SQL health before returning to read-write mode.
summary: On January 24, 2017, the Stack Exchange network experienced a critical outage due to a bugcheck on the primary SQL Server, causing a read-only state and subsequent downtime. The issue was resolved by restarting the SQL service and performing health checks.

title: FirstEnergy / General Electric incident
description: FirstEnergy had a local failure when some transmission lines hit untrimmed foliage. The normal process is to have an alarm go off, which causes human operators to re-distribute power. But the GE system that was monitoring this had a bug which prevented the alarm from getting triggered, which eventually caused a cascading failure that eventually affected 55 million people.
symptoms: Alarm system failure prevented operators from redistributing power after transmission lines dropped in voltage.
root_cause: Software bug in the alarm system at the control room of FirstEnergy.
resolution: Not specified
summary: The Northeast blackout of 2003 was caused by a software bug in FirstEnergy's alarm system, which failed to alert operators to redistribute power, leading to a cascading failure that affected 55 million people. The incident lasted between 2 hours and 4 days, with full restoration taking several days.

title: Zerodha incident
description: A failure of the primary leased line to a CTCL between a stock broker and a stock exchange led to the activation of a backup leased line that was operating sporadically over the following hour, affecting bracket and cover orders. Subsequently, the process of placing and validating orders had been modified to incorporate the unreliability of the CTCL's leased lines, but the reliability of the primary and the backup leased lines was not fundamentally improved by the providers.
symptoms: Hanging orders and positions, orders not getting placed on the exchanges, client complaints regarding bracket and cover orders.
root_cause: Failure of the primary leased line and subsequent unreliability of the backup leased line.
resolution: Shut off the affected CTCL to prevent new orders, lapsed and reconciled pending orders with the exchange, planned a new release to improve exit options for bracket and cover orders.
summary: The incident involved hanging orders due to the failure of a primary leased line and an unreliable backup, affecting a small percentage of clients. The affected CTCL was shut down to prevent further issues, and a new release is planned to enhance order exit options.

title: Discord incident
description: A flapping service lead to a thundering herd reconnecting to it once it came up. This lead to a cascading error where frontend services ran out of memory due to internal queues filling up.
symptoms: Dramatic degradation in service, OOM errors, and crashes in the sessions cluster.
root_cause: A flapping service led to a thundering herd reconnecting, causing frontend services to run out of memory due to internal queues filling up.
resolution: Engineers implemented code patches to limit connections and message sending, followed by a global reboot of Discord.
duration: From 11:38 to 14:07, approximately 2 hours and 29 minutes.
summary: Discord experienced severe service degradation due to a cascading failure triggered by a flapping service, leading to memory exhaustion in the sessions cluster. Engineers deployed patches and executed a global reboot to restore service functionality.

title: Stack Exchange incident
description: Enabling StackEgg for all users resulted in heavy load on load balancers and consequently, a DDoS.
symptoms: Heavy load on load balancers, resulting in a 6 minute public outage on Stack Overflow.
root_cause: Enabling StackEgg for all users led to a significant increase in concurrent sessions due to additional AJAX requests.
resolution: Raised the concurrent sessions limit in HAProxy and reduced the number of HTTP requests from StackEgg by embedding data directly in the page and increasing dependence on websockets.
summary: The incident was caused by enabling StackEgg for all users, which resulted in excessive load on load balancers and a 6 minute outage. The issue was resolved by increasing the concurrent sessions limit and optimizing HTTP requests.

title: Cloudflare incident
description: A parser bug caused Cloudflare edge servers to return memory that contained private information such as HTTP cookies, authentication tokens, HTTP POST bodies, and other sensitive data.
symptoms: Corrupted web pages being returned by HTTP requests through Cloudflare.
root_cause: A parser bug caused edge servers to return memory past the end of a buffer, leaking private information.
resolution: Turned off three minor Cloudflare features using the same HTML parser chain, preventing memory from being returned in HTTP responses.
duration: The greatest period of impact was from February 13 to February 18, with a fix implemented globally in under 7 hours.
summary: Cloudflare experienced corrupted web pages due to a parser bug that leaked private information. The issue was resolved by disabling affected features, with a global fix implemented in under 7 hours.

title: Homebrew incident
description: A GitHub personal access token with recently elevated scopes was leaked from Homebrew’s Jenkins that allowed access to `git push` on several Homebrew repositories.
symptoms: A GitHub personal access token was leaked, allowing unauthorized access to git push on Homebrew repositories.
root_cause: A GitHub personal access token with recently elevated scopes was leaked from Homebrew’s Jenkins.
resolution: The leaked credentials were revoked, replaced, and sanitised within Jenkins; branch protection and required reviews were enabled on repositories.
summary: A GitHub personal access token leak from Homebrew’s Jenkins allowed unauthorized git push access. The incident was resolved by revoking and replacing the token, and implementing branch protection and review requirements.

resolution: Engineers rolled back to the last known good configuration and suspended customer-initiated configuration changes to validate the fix.
summary: Google Cloud Networking experienced issues with the Google Cloud Load Balancing service, resulting in 404 errors for customers due to a latent bug in the configuration service. The issue was resolved by rolling back to a stable configuration and suspending changes to prevent recurrence.

title: Twilio incident
description: In 2013, a temporary network partition in the redis cluster used for billing operations, caused a massive resynchronization from slaves. The overloaded master crashed and when it was restarted, it started up in read-only mode. The auto-recharge component in This resulted in failed transactions from Twilio's auto-recharge service, which unfortunately billed the customers before updating their balance internally. So the auto-recharge system continued to retry the transaction again and again, resulting in multiple charges to customer's credit cards.
symptoms: Erroneous credit card charges, account suspensions, delayed usage reports, and failure of services relying on the redis-master.
root_cause: A temporary network partition in the redis cluster caused a massive resynchronization from slaves, leading to an overloaded master that crashed and restarted in read-only mode.
resolution: The redis-master was restarted to address the high load, and the billing system was taken offline to prevent further erroneous charges. Refunds for erroneous charges were processed.
summary: Twilio's billing system experienced erroneous credit card charges and account suspensions due to a network partition in the redis cluster, which overloaded the master. The issue was resolved by restarting the redis-master and taking the billing system offline to prevent further charges.

title: Joyent incident
description: An operator used a tool with lax input validation to reboot a small number of servers undergoing maintenance but forgot to type `-n` and instead rebooted all servers in the datacenter. This caused an outage that lasted 2.5 hours, rebooted all customer instances, put tremendous load on DHCP/TFTP PXE boot systems, and left API systems requiring manual intervention. See also [Bryan Cantrill's talk](https://www.youtube.com/watch?v=30jNsCVLpAE).
symptoms: All us-east-1 API systems and customer instances were simultaneously rebooted, causing downtime and load on DHCP/TFTP PXE boot systems.
root_cause: An operator used a tool with lax input validation and forgot to type `-n`, resulting in the reboot of all servers in the datacenter.
resolution: The API was restored and service was fully available after manual intervention.
summary: An operator error led to the simultaneous reboot of all us-east-1 API systems and customer instances, causing a 2.5-hour outage. The issue was resolved with manual intervention to restore service.

title: CCP Games incident
description: A typo and a name conflict caused the installer to sometimes delete the *boot.ini* file on installation of an expansion for *EVE Online* - with [consequences.](https://www.youtube.com/watch?v=msXRFJ2ar_E)
symptoms: Players reported that the installer was deleting the C:\boot.ini file, causing some computers to fail to start until the file was fixed.
root_cause: A typo in the installer script led to the deletion of the boot.ini file from the root of the current drive instead of the intended directory.
resolution: The installer script was corrected to ensure that the full path was specified to prevent the deletion of critical system files.
summary: The EVE Online expansion installer caused critical issues by deleting the C:\boot.ini file, leading to startup failures for some players. The root cause was a typo in the installer script, which was subsequently fixed by specifying the full path in the deletion command.

title: Platform.sh incident
description: Outage during a scheduled maintenance window because there were too much data for Zookeeper to boot.
symptoms: Websites went down, gateways couldn't fetch the list of applications, and logs showed random connection drops to ZooKeeper.
root_cause: The orchestration software failed to fully start due to a bug in the Kazoo library, resulting in EPIPE errors when connecting to ZooKeeper.
resolution: Identified the bug in the Kazoo library and resolved the connection issues to ZooKeeper.
summary: During a scheduled maintenance, websites went down due to the orchestration software not fully starting, caused by a bug in the Kazoo library managing ZooKeeper connections. The issue was resolved by fixing the connection errors.

title: Google incident
description: A mail system emailed people more than 20 times. This happened because mail was sent with a batch cron job that sent mail to everyone who was marked as waiting for mail. This was a non-atomic operation and the batch job didn't mark people as not waiting until all messages were sent.
symptoms: The automated email system sent an email more than 20 times to a large number of registrants from 2013, leading to user complaints.
root_cause: A bug in the refactoring of the mail system caused a non-atomic operation where the batch job didn't mark users as not waiting until all messages were sent.
resolution: Not specified
summary: The Code Jam email system malfunctioned, resulting in repeated emails sent to users due to a bug in the mail system's refactoring. The issue stemmed from a non-atomic operation in the batch job that failed to update user statuses correctly.
